sound_type: quiet grunts during courtship head-throw display -- low ka-KAA grunt at apex of backward head-throw, 200-1500 Hz, audible only at close range (10-30 meters), visual-primary acoustic-secondary display system, E=3
ecosystem: Mountain lake -- breeds on high-elevation freshwater lakes in Cascades and Northern Rockies (500-2000m). Clear deep lakes with rocky/gravelly bottoms in coniferous forest zones. Winters on coastal waters and lowland lakes in Puget Sound region.
season: Year-round in PNW. Altitudinal migrant -- breeds on mountain lakes in summer, descends to coastal and lowland waters in winter. Courtship displays most intense late winter through spring.

coordinates.description: Reference for mountain lake breeding habitat in the central Cascades. Barrow's Goldeneye breeds on high-elevation lakes throughout the Cascade Range.

taxonomy_note.description: Sixth consecutive Anatidae (118-123). Second of the Bucephala trilogy (Bufflehead 122, Barrow's 123, Common 124). First of the comparison pair with Common Goldeneye (degree 124). Named after Sir John Barrow (1764-1848), Second Secretary of the Admiralty. Species name islandica refers to Iceland where first described, but population stronghold is western North America.
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Anatidae count: 11+ entries (Harlequin Duck 118, Common Merganser 119, Hooded Merganser 120, Wood Duck 121, Bufflehead 122, Barrow's Goldeneye 123, plus earlier entries)
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Bucephala from Greek bous ('ox') + kephale ('head') -- 'ox-headed,' referring to the large, rounded head shape. islandica from Iceland, where the species was first formally described. Common name 'Barrow's' honors Sir John Barrow.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Bucephala -- second entry after Bufflehead (122). Third entry at degree 124 (Common Goldeneye). The Bucephala trilogy.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Anatidae continues for a sixth consecutive degree. Approaching the Picidae nonet record (9 consecutive). Degree 124 (Common Goldeneye) would make seven.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: 200-1500 Hz. Low-frequency grunts concentrated in the lower vocal spectrum. Not designed for long-distance communication.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: 300-800 Hz. The courtship grunt concentrates energy in the low-mid frequencies.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Male: ka-KAA grunt at 200-800 Hz. Female: harsh gack/crr at 400-1500 Hz.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Individual grunt: 0.3-0.5 seconds. Head-throw display sequence: approximately 1.5 seconds total (including physical motion).
song_metrics.note_structure: COURTSHIP GRUNT: Low ka-KAA delivered at apex of head-throw. Quiet, guttural. Female: harsh gack, low-volume. Both calls subordinate to visual display.
song_metrics.note_types: Male courtship grunt (ka-KAA, head-throw), female harsh call (gack/crr), alarm notes. All low-volume.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Single grunts per head-throw. Multiple head-throws in a display bout. No extended vocal sequences -- each grunt accompanies one physical display.
song_metrics.timbre: Guttural. Low. Grunting. The sound is more felt than heard at distance. Not musical or resonant -- purely functional vocalization accompanying a visual display.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=3. Low volume. Audible at 10-30 meters in calm conditions. The visual display (head-throw) carries information farther than the acoustic component. Visual-primary, acoustic-secondary display system.
song_metrics.tempo: Head-throw displays occur in bouts -- multiple males displaying simultaneously. Individual display rate: 3-6 head-throws per minute during active courtship. Irregular timing -- responsive to female attention and male competition.
song_metrics.context: COURTSHIP AND PAIR BONDING. The head-throw display is performed by males in groups, directed at females. Multiple males display simultaneously. The quiet grunt marks the apex of the physical display. Also used for pair-bond maintenance between established pairs.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Minimal vocal variation across range. Display behavior is consistent.
song_metrics.key_property: QUIET GRUNTS DISPLAY -- visual-primary courtship. E=3. Low ka-KAA grunt at apex of dramatic head-throw. Audible only at close range. The display is visually spectacular (heads snapping backward, water splashing, bodies rising) but acoustically understated. Mountain lake specialist. Sixth consecutive Anatidae (118-123). First of comparison pair with Common Goldeneye (124). Crescent facial patch diagnostic.

plumage.overall: Male: black and white diving duck with distinctive CRESCENT-SHAPED white facial patch between eye and bill. Head has purplish gloss in good light. Back predominantly black. White scapular patches less extensive than Common Goldeneye. Female: brown head, gray body, yellow-tipped bill.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: Male: CRESCENT-SHAPED white facial patch (vs. Common Goldeneye's round spot). Purplish head gloss (vs. Common's greenish). More angular, blocky head shape (vs. Common's rounder). Female: shorter, more triangular bill with more extensive yellow-orange; steeper forehead.
plumage.seasonal_variation: Eclipse plumage in summer -- males resemble females. Non-breeding plumage less distinctive. Identification harder outside breeding season.
plumage.flight_pattern: Fast, direct flight. Rapid wingbeats. Produces a wing-whistle (less pronounced than Common Goldeneye). Compact, heavy-bodied profile in flight.

physical_description.overall: Medium-sized diving duck. Body length 42-50 cm. Wingspan 67-76 cm. Weight 600-1300 g. Slightly smaller on average than Common Goldeneye but overlap is substantial.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: Crescent-shaped white facial patch on male. Steeper forehead and more angular head than Common Goldeneye. Both structural and plumage characters needed for confident identification.
physical_description.field_id: Look for: crescent (not round) white facial patch on male; purplish (not greenish) head gloss; steeper, more angular forehead; shorter, more triangular bill on female. In winter mixed flocks on Puget Sound, compare head shape and facial patch shape directly.

nodes.title: Mountain Lake Specialist -- Northwestern Specialty
nodes.description: Breeds on high-elevation Cascade and Rocky Mountain lakes (500-2000m). 150,000-200,000 globally, concentrated in western North America + Iceland. A fundamentally northwestern bird with limited global range.

nodes.title: The Crescent vs. The Circle -- Barrow's vs. Common Identification
nodes.description: Barrow's: crescent-shaped facial patch, purplish head gloss, angular head. Common: round spot, greenish gloss, rounder head. Classic birding identification challenge. Mixed winter flocks on Puget Sound.

nodes.title: Cavity Nester -- Pileated Woodpecker Connection
nodes.description: Secondary cavity nester using old Pileated Woodpecker and Northern Flicker excavations near mountain lakes. Dead snag economy (degree 109) provides nest sites. Ducklings jump from cavity to forest floor.

nodes.title: Bucephala Trilogy -- Three Ox-Headed Divers (122-124)
nodes.description: Bufflehead (122, smallest), Barrow's Goldeneye (123, most restricted range), Common Goldeneye (124, most widespread). Three Bucephala species in sequence. Genus name means 'ox-headed.'

nodes.title: Sixth Consecutive Anatidae (118-123)
nodes.description: Harlequin (118), Common Merganser (119), Hooded Merganser (120), Wood Duck (121), Bufflehead (122), Barrow's Goldeneye (123). Six consecutive. Approaching Picidae nonet record (9).

cross_references.relationship: COMPARISON PAIR! Barrow's (crescent, mountain, E=3, northwestern specialty) vs. Common (round spot, widespread, E=4, wing-whistle). Same genus Bucephala. The crescent vs. the circle. Mirrors Hairy/Downy Woodpecker pair (110-111).
BUCEPHALA TRILOGY! Bufflehead (122), Barrow's Goldeneye (123), Common Goldeneye (124). Three Bucephala species. Smallest (Bufflehead), most restricted (Barrow's), most widespread (Common).
CAVITY CONNECTION! Barrow's Goldeneye nests in old Pileated Woodpecker cavities. The dead snag economy (degree 109) provides nest sites for goldeneye young. Cross-family ecological link.
CAVITY NESTING! Both cavity-nesting Anatidae. Wood Duck (forest pools, E=3) and Barrow's (mountain lakes, E=3). Same nesting strategy, different lake types. Both depend on Pileated cavities.
ANATIDAE STREAK! Harlequin (118) started the streak. Six consecutive Anatidae at degree 123. Stream specialist to mountain lake specialist. Approaching Picidae nonet record.
